The Creation of the World.definition: Belonging to the very earliest beginnings of the world — ancient beyond any memory.
The Creation of the World.context: Chaos was primordial — it existed before any god, any light, any shape, or any name.

The Creation of the World.definition: Having supreme power and authority — the ruler who answers to no one above them.
The Creation of the World.context: Cronus became sovereign of all the gods after overthrowing his father Uranus.

The Creation of the World.definition: A prediction of what will happen, often delivered by an oracle or god — and usually impossible to avoid.
The Creation of the World.context: The prophecy that haunted Cronus was simple and terrible: a child of his would overthrow him.

The Twelve Olympians.definition: A god or powerful person who especially protects and supports a particular place, craft, or group of people.
The Twelve Olympians.context: Athena was the patron of Athens — she gave it law, weaving, the olive tree, and her name.

The Twelve Olympians.definition: The area of the world, or field of life, that a god rules over and is responsible for.
The Twelve Olympians.context: Each Olympian had their domain: Zeus the sky, Poseidon the sea, Hades the underworld.

The Twelve Olympians.definition: Inspiring respect, awe, or fear — impressive in a way that makes you take them seriously.
The Twelve Olympians.context: Hera was formidable — she was fierce, long-memoried, and never let an injustice go.

Prometheus and the Gift of Fire.definition: Thinking carefully about the future before acting — planning ahead for consequences.
Prometheus and the Gift of Fire.context: Prometheus's name literally meant forethought: he had sided with Zeus because he had thought it through.

Prometheus and the Gift of Fire.definition: Openly refusing to obey a rule or authority — going against what someone powerful has commanded.
Prometheus and the Gift of Fire.context: Prometheus's gift of fire to humanity was an act of defiance against Zeus's direct command.

Prometheus and the Gift of Fire.definition: Punishment or hardship accepted as payment for a wrong — something done or endured to make amends.
Prometheus and the Gift of Fire.context: Prometheus paid his penance on the mountain for centuries, the eagle returning every morning.

Pandora's Box.definition: A rule that forbids something — a clear instruction not to do a specific thing.
Pandora's Box.context: The prohibition was simple: do not open the jar. It was the only rule, and it was the impossible one.

Pandora's Box.definition: Certain to happen — impossible to avoid no matter what you do.
Pandora's Box.context: Pandora's curiosity and the sealed jar were a combination that felt inevitable from the moment they were in the same room.

Pandora's Box.definition: Continuing to work at something difficult without giving up, even over a long time.
Pandora's Box.context: Hera gave Pandora perseverance as a gift — the ability to keep going even when things are hard.

To take someone away by force, against t.definition: An agreement where both sides give up something to reach a solution neither would have fully chosen.
To take someone away by force, against t.context: The arrangement reached was a compromise: part of the year above ground, part below — no one got everything they wanted.

To take someone away by force, against t.definition: To become less strict or harsh — to give in after a period of refusing.
To take someone away by force, against t.context: Zeus finally relented and sent Hermes to retrieve Persephone, but only after the earth was dying.

Orpheus and Eurydice.definition: So grief-stricken that nothing can comfort you — beyond the reach of ordinary comfort.
Orpheus and Eurydice.context: Orpheus was inconsolable after Eurydice died — he could not eat, sleep, or play music without her.

Orpheus and Eurydice.definition: To force something to happen — to make someone do something through irresistible power or persuasion.
Orpheus and Eurydice.context: Orpheus's music could compel anything: rivers stopped, rocks moved, the Furies wept.

Orpheus and Eurydice.definition: Going down — especially into something deep, dangerous, or dark.
Orpheus and Eurydice.context: The descent into the underworld was the part that no living person had ever survived — Orpheus did it anyway.

Icarus and the Wings of Wax.definition: Dangerous overconfidence — the kind of pride that makes you ignore warnings and reach too far.
Icarus and the Wings of Wax.context: Flying higher toward the sun when his father had said middle height — that was hubris, and it cost Icarus everything.

Icarus and the Wings of Wax.definition: Working through a problem carefully and in order, one step at a time.
Icarus and the Wings of Wax.context: Daedalus was methodical: he collected feathers by size, shaped wings carefully, and explained every control before launch.

Icarus and the Wings of Wax.definition: An intense feeling of excitement and aliveness — a rushing joy, often from something fast or thrilling.
Icarus and the Wings of Wax.context: The exhilaration of flight was what undid Icarus — the sky was extraordinary, and he climbed toward more of it.

Midas and the Golden Touch.definition: An intense, excessive greed for wealth — wanting more and more, never satisfied.
Midas and the Golden Touch.context: Midas didn't have cruel avarice — he just loved gold with an enthusiasm that made him terrible at wishing.

Midas and the Golden Touch.definition: The result of an action — what happens because of a choice, especially an unexpected or negative result.
Midas and the Golden Touch.context: The consequence of the golden touch arrived at dinner: bread turned to gold, wine turned to gold, his daughter turned to gold.

Midas and the Golden Touch.definition: To take back something that was granted — to cancel or undo an agreement or gift.
Midas and the Golden Touch.context: Dionysus allowed Midas to revoke his wish by washing in the river Pactolus — the gold drained away into the water.

Perseus and Medusa.definition: To turn to stone — or, in a wider sense, to freeze someone with extreme terror.
Perseus and Medusa.context: Anyone who looked directly at Medusa would be petrified — turned to stone instantly, permanently.

Perseus and Medusa.definition: Able to find clever solutions to difficult problems using whatever is available.
Perseus and Medusa.context: Perseus was resourceful: no horse, no plan, no money — but a polished shield, a bag, winged sandals, and a cap of invisibility.

Perseus and Medusa.definition: Acting too quickly without thinking, in a way that creates problems.
Perseus and Medusa.context: Perseus's rash promise — 'I'll bring whatever is asked, even Medusa's head' — was the thing that started the whole quest.

Theseus and the Minotaur.definition: A payment — often in people, treasure, or goods — made by a weaker power to a stronger one as a sign of submission.
Theseus and the Minotaur.context: Athens sent fourteen young people as tribute to Crete every nine years, because it had no choice.

Theseus and the Minotaur.definition: A complex maze designed to be impossible to navigate — something you can enter but cannot find your way out of.
Theseus and the Minotaur.context: The Labyrinth was Daedalus's masterpiece: once you were inside, the design worked systematically against you.

Theseus and the Minotaur.definition: Here: Ariadne's device — a literal thread tied at the entrance, unrolled as a guide, the only reliable way back out.
Theseus and the Minotaur.context: The thread was the solution the Labyrinth couldn't defeat: not a weapon, not strength — just a line back to where you started.

Heracles and the Twelve Labors.definition: Suffering or labor undertaken to make amends for a wrong — doing hard things as payment for harm caused.
Heracles and the Twelve Labors.context: The twelve labors were Heracles's penance — the Oracle's prescription for what he had to do before he could be free.

Herculean.definition: To keep going despite obstacles, difficulty, and failure — to continue when stopping would be easier.
Herculean.context: Every labor was designed to stop Heracles. He persevered through all twelve, including the ones that required asking for help.

Jason and the Golden Fleece.definition: A journey undertaken by a group for a specific, often dangerous purpose.
Jason and the Golden Fleece.context: The voyage of the Argo was the greatest expedition of the age — fifty heroes, one ship, the far edge of the world.

Jason and the Golden Fleece.definition: A solemn promise, often sworn by the gods — considered absolutely binding.
Jason and the Golden Fleece.context: Jason swore an oath to Medea by the gods; he later broke it, and the gods did not forget.

Jason and the Golden Fleece.definition: Breaking someone's trust in a serious way — especially after they have given you everything.
Jason and the Golden Fleece.context: Jason's betrayal of Medea — setting her aside for a political marriage — was the act the story refused to forgive.

Atalanta and the Golden Apples.definition: A specific condition or requirement attached to an agreement.
Atalanta and the Golden Apples.context: Atalanta's stipulation was clear: outrun her and win her hand; lose and pay with your life.

Atalanta and the Golden Apples.definition: Something that draws your attention away from what you should be focused on.
Atalanta and the Golden Apples.context: The golden apples were a distraction — Atalanta knew it, and paused for them anyway.

Atalanta and the Golden Apples.definition: The feeling of being thankful — and the importance of expressing that thankfulness.
Atalanta and the Golden Apples.context: Hippomenes forgot to show gratitude to Aphrodite for the apples, which proved to be a costly oversight.

Athena and Arachne.definition: To speak with excessive pride about your own abilities — saying you're better than others in a way that invites trouble.
Athena and Arachne.context: Arachne's boast — that she would challenge Athena and win — was what brought the goddess to her door.

Echo and Narcissus.definition: Excessive pride in your own appearance or abilities — caring too much about how you seem to others.
Echo and Narcissus.context: Narcissus's vanity was so complete that he fell in love with his own reflection and could not look away.

Echo and Narcissus.definition: Being so focused on something that you can't think about anything else, even when it's harmful.
Echo and Narcissus.context: His obsession with his reflection kept him at the water's edge until he died — it was all he wanted, and he couldn't have it.

Echo and Narcissus.definition: To gradually disappear or become less — to weaken or diminish over time.
Echo and Narcissus.context: Echo faded until nothing was left of her but her voice — still in the mountains, still repeating the last words she heard.

Odysseus and the Cyclops.definition: Clever in a sly way — using intelligence and deception to get what you need.
Odysseus and the Cyclops.context: Odysseus's cunning gave him the solution: Nobody as a name, wine to dull the giant, a stake hardened in the coals.

Odysseus and the Cyclops.definition: The generous and welcoming treatment of guests — in Greek culture, a sacred duty protected by Zeus himself.
Odysseus and the Cyclops.context: Odysseus appealed to the laws of hospitality; Polyphemus ignored them, which told Odysseus everything about who he was dealing with.

Odysseus and the Cyclops.definition: Dangerous pride — the overconfidence that makes you show off when staying quiet would have saved you.
Odysseus and the Cyclops.context: Odysseus's hubris was shouting his real name as they escaped — it was the mistake that cost him ten years.
